62377282

this is why i'm not fundamentally against protectionism. in a global world there's always an arms race of who can provide the most "business-friendly" (read: low tax) environment. but even if you think benefits shouldnt be a thing at all, a country needs roads, infrastructure, a military ... it's corrosive to the wellbeing and autonomy of a nation. if you drop your tax to zero you'll attract business in the short term, until a bunch of countries do it, and then your advantage is gone and the state is broke.

ive had this debate with my dad like 20 years ago when discount stores (groceries mostly, but also everything else really) used to pop up everywhere here and he was super excited about the bargain deals. it seemed pretty obvious to me even then that a discounter that, all else being equal, will have lower profit margins will pay their employees less and only make a handful people at the top rich by eating into everyone else's market share. even if you're not in retail that mentality will spread if unchecked and bite a lot of people in the ass. you're creating a situation where most everyone is poor and living of formerly discount (now barely affordable) groceries while a couple extremely wealthy persons are mainlining caviar.

it's a delicate affair and tariffs tend to do more harm than good but i wouldnt mind living in a world where things life food production are mostly local and only the shit where economies of (vast) scale are necessary and local production unfeasible (like chips) is traded globally.

i'm really not all that opposed the heavy handed state interventionism of the early 1900s USA or modern china if it's done wisely (thats a big "if", i know)

I for one propose we spend our time learning a subject. Knowledge is the greatest wealth of all.

However, I would be remiss to not tell the truth that I haven't a clue what we should study.

So instead I suggest we create an eggregore for these threads. Others made monero-chan, bobo and sminem

I am thinking we create one based on a girl who would be willing to believe in us while we are poor and brave the pain throughout having to witness our more disappointing moments of action. Never losing faith in us, she is always there. Yes. This is how we shall spend our time.

At any rate, that is my suggestion for tonight's fun. I have to work in the morning so let's not get too rowdy. Take it or leave it. If you will take it, then be sure to double it and pass it on to the next anon.

Let us commence the general!

Come one, come all!

item.title: PSA for SpaceX baggies — 22 replies — 88.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
PSA for SpaceX baggies

Open thread • Replies: 22 • Updated: 2026-06-16 22:15 UTC

OP

Reminder:
The moon is made of poison.
Unfathomably small shards of jagged micro-glass that have never has their razor edges dulled by wind or rain that eat through Kevlar space suit like abrasive fluid.

It gets everywhere, and if you accidently inhaled a cloud of it, you will die, either shortly, or later due to its carcinogenic chemical properties (its also radioactive in the extreme).

Neither the moon or the mars have a living core.
This means:
>A) Anything of value like gold or silver or tantalum or palladium sank to the core a billion years ago
>B) Any atmosphere you terraform with nukes (retarded) would blow away. (Obviously, you cannot re-light a planets core, if you have that level of energy, you can just open a wormhole to another earth.)

Asteroid mining is a meme.
You cannot mine asteroids anymore today with Falcon 9 than you could 40 years ago.

Roids (with heavy metals) are protoplanets, like mars and the moon their cores are dead, and that is where all the heavy rare earths are.

You cant just land Curiosity on the surface and have it sweep up the gold and iridium into a bin.

So you're either drilling to the core in space autonomously through a km of rock and iron hoping a gas pocket doesn't blow (not possible on earth autonomously), or you're crashing one here.

In either case, you need the fuel to get a skyscrapers weight in robotic mining equpment to the roid to drill a km deep, or you need the fuel to move a skyscraper.This would be more fuel than has ever been launched, and you'd need fuel to get that fuel up there.

Won't waste time on datacenter memes, theres a 4 second delay to the moon and back, on top of normal internet speed delays, and tons of noise.
If you want to spend 3x the gdp of europe building dial up on the moon be my guest.

Hope this helps.
Hope you don't lose your shit due to unintentional ignorance.
If you do now, its willful ignorance.

Early replies (first 3)

62377346
>>62377342

62377349

62377361
The bean counters told me we literally could not afford to buy seven dollars worth of moon rocks, much less seventy million. Bought 'em anyway. Engineers said the moon rocks were too volatile to experiment on. Tested on 'em anyway. Ground 'em up, mixed 'em into a gel. And guess what? Ground up moon rocks are pure poison. I am deathly ill. Still, it turns out they're a great portal conductor.

62377364

62377374

62377393

62377394
>>62377351
A typical small 200-meter asteroid (not the kind that have anything of value, but I'm using this for effect) typically has a mass between 4 million to 10 million metric tons.

To change the velocity (the trajectory) of one by 1 (one) meter per second, you would need roughly 10 billion kilograms of rocket fuel using current best in class high efficiency motors.

SpaceX has lowered the cost per kilo to $3000ish.

7 × 109 kg × $3,000/kg = $21, 000, 0000, 000, 0000
>21 TRILLION DOLLARS
The GDP of America for a year.
to move a small roid by 1m/sec

In reality you would need to divert it by at a minimum 100m/sec on the absolute best case close orbital path, or the sum total of humanities GDP throughout the 20th century for a single roid.

Hope this makes it clear.
You're not mining roids.

item.title: I talked to two people today about rent. — 137 replies — 510.6/hr
item.content:encoded:
I talked to two people today about rent.

Open thread • Replies: 137 • Updated: 2026-06-16 22:10 UTC

OP

I talked to two people today about rent.

The first guy said he lives in Atlanta and pays about $2,300 a month in rent. I mentioned that works out to roughly $27,600 a year, which I personally think is a lot for rent. He also told me he was let go from a sales job at a small company about two weeks ago. Small company = low pay. When I commented on the rent being high, he responded by saying, “maybe it is a lot for you.”

I told him it is a terrible financial decision.
I also told him that I live in a major city and my monthly taxes alone are more than that.

Later, I spoke with another guy who pays around $2,000 a month for a luxury apartment. That’s about $24,000 a year. I didn’t ask about his income or job situation, but I shared that I thought the annual rent cost seemed very high.

I kept telling him his rent was a waste of money and he should buy a house. He told me he might not want to to live somewhere for so long. I asked him how he expected to build wealth. The conversation went back and forth with me saying it was a waste of “money down the drain.” He eventually admitted it was hard and bad use of money and insulted me with “life is hard when YOU don’t live in mommies basement.”
Wow.
He is literally throwing money down the drain and I trying to give me good advice.

It made me wonder if people commonly spend over $20,000–$30,000 a year on rent? And if so, how common is that relative to income and lifestyle choices?

Please look at picrel. Do people spend so much of their paycheque on rent? How does anyone become rich?

Curious what others think about this.

>>62372604
When you really think about it, home owners are losing a lot of money too. First there's the down payment, that money grows a lot faster in stocks. In some cases it may be worth more than the entire value of the house in 30 years. Housing simply can't compete with the gains from the stock market. Secondly, even if you own your home and it's a 600k asset. Living in it, is throwing money away. If you sold you could be making 60k a year from your investments. Renting for 30k a year would be a net gain.

Home owners are mostly idiots, unless they are landlords or rich and retired. It is the handicap that prevents high earners from ever making it.

62376833
>>62372604
It depends on the market of course but yeah $2300 is not high in most places these days. If you equate that to a house payment that is under $300k which in major metros is ghetto tier housing. There is nothing inherently wrong with renting from a financial standpoint, even Dave Ramsey would agree. If you are not planning to stay put for 5-10 years then you are better off renting. The transaction costs for home ownership are quite high, often 10% of the price. The most important factor is, as your picrel states, the percentage of your income you spend on housing. If you want to invest and get rich, stay under 25% of your take-home.

This will be one of the greatest investment opportunities in our lifetime within another decade or so.

Tech is stagnating, just as every other industry is out of viable optimization methodologies. There is nothing new being created, every niche is saturated and optimized. AI will produce some value but it is ultimately a giant bubble that will never, and I mean never advance to the point of consciousness or intelligence (unless they use biological brains and do so correctly - they will fail). Science doesn't even know where consciousness comes.

That being said: sex robots and eventual artificial womb tech (much easier than actually creating consciousness) will be the big new technology that solves real world problems. And the funny thing is: tech companies will eventually run out of other means of generating increasing revenue and will essentially HAVE to go this route, just like they're currently doing with AI. They have nothing else. This technology will have vast and continuous demand as it will entirely replace the old/current paradigm.
